title: Pillar Summary Context
summary: Richmond's small business support infrastructure is fragmented across OMBD, RVA Business Portal, and nonprofit partners, creating navigation barriers for entrepreneurs and contractors seeking to establish or grow businesses. The city lacks a coordinated front door for business formation questions, forcing users to discover services through scattered information channels.

title: Master Research Prompt
summary: Evidence-based software opportunities aligned with Mayor's Action Plan Pillars 4 (thriving economy) and 7 (civic infrastructure) should address information access, navigation, and coordination barriers in small business formation and government contracting. The research foundation prioritizes user problems over technical sophistication and emphasizes responsible deployment of civic technology.

title: Top 10 Research Questions
summary: Key research questions focus on understanding Richmond's contracting information ecosystem, barriers to MBE participation, availability of support infrastructure, and sufficiency of public data for civic technology solutions. Questions examine both information availability and user awareness of existing services.

title: Top 10 Hypotheses to Test
summary: Testable hypotheses address how information presentation, plain language explanations, and public data availability influence user navigation and contracting success. Hypotheses prioritize navigation confusion and information clarity as primary friction points in business and contracting workflows.

title: Red Flags
summary: Persistent disparities in MBE contracting utilization, lack of documented staff knowledge, and unsustainable support infrastructure represent critical red flags suggesting systemic rather than informational barriers. Documentation gaps and institutional fragility create risks for any technology-dependent solution.

title: Missing Information Gaps
summary: Critical gaps exist in institutional continuity, technical infrastructure, stakeholder documentation, and regulatory navigation guidance across city and state agencies. These gaps expose risks of institutional dependence and create uncertainty in business formation pathways.

title: Problem Landscape: MBE Contracting
summary: MBE contracting in Richmond faces documented disparity (3.43% utilization vs 17.93% availability) rooted in information asymmetries, contracting vehicle confusion, and access barriers to bid opportunities. Systemic factors including SWaM certification complexity and fragmented procurement information create friction beyond simple awareness.

title: Problem Landscape: Small Business Journey
summary: Small business formation in Richmond requires navigation across fragmented city, state, and federal regulatory requirements with unclear sequencing and interdependencies. Certificate of Zoning Compliance prerequisites and business registration steps create confusion due to undocumented process relationships.

title: Problem Landscape: Evaluation Framework
summary: An evaluation framework for problem statement readiness examines urgency, buildability, information quality, and user pain to distinguish genuine opportunities from assumed problems. The framework prioritizes evidence-based problem validation over intuition.

title: Problem Landscape: Problem Types Classification
summary: Business and contracting problems can be classified as information-access problems, navigation problems, interpretation problems, or institutional process problems, each requiring different solution approaches. Classification prevents misapplication of solutions to wrong problem types.

title: Problem Landscape: Root Causes
summary: Five primary root cause categories underlie business and contracting difficulties: lack of data, lack of clarity, lack of sequence, lack of confidence, and lack of trusted front door. These categories enable precise problem diagnosis and targeted solution design.

title: User Personas: Contracting
summary: Five distinct MBE contractor personas (Marcus-specialty trade, Keisha-first-time contractor, David-specialty subcontractor, Anita-service-oriented, James-emerging construction) represent different contracting pathways with distinct information needs and pain points. Personas reveal that first-time contractors face fundamentally different barriers than established operators.

title: User Personas: Business Setup
summary: Primary small business formation personas like Sarah Chen (home-based service provider) face recurring questions about business structure, pricing strategy, credibility establishment, and growth pathways. Persona analysis reveals patterns in information-seeking behavior and decision points.

title: User Stakeholders: Map
summary: Comprehensive institutional actor mapping identifies stakeholders across Mayor's Action Plan pillars including city departments, nonprofit partners, educational institutions, and workforce development entities. Stakeholder interdependencies and influence networks shape service delivery and information architecture.

title: User Stakeholders: Priorities
summary: Stakeholder priority analysis reveals constituencies prioritize reduced staff burden, improved public information, increased MBE participation, fewer abandoned registrations, and better process sequencing. Priorities reveal tension between different stakeholder groups requiring design tradeoffs.

title: User Staff Knowledge
summary: Approximately 80% of organizational knowledge about business processes exists tacitly in staff practitioners rather than formal documentation, creating institutional fragility and public access barriers. This knowledge asymmetry represents both a critical constraint and opportunity for technology-enabled documentation.

title: Services Landscape: Existing
summary: Richmond's service landscape includes OMBD (1500 East Main St), RVA Business Portal (launched January 2025), Office of Community Wealth Building, and nonprofit partners providing fragmented business formation and contracting support. Service fragmentation creates navigation confusion despite comprehensive service availability.

title: Services Landscape: Contracting Support
summary: Virginia state-level SWaM certification and SBSD programs integrate with Richmond-specific services including OMBD vendor registration, OpenGov platform, and BidNet Direct. Multi-layered service ecosystem creates complexity in understanding available pathways and requirements.

title: Services Landscape: Front Door
summary: Twelve primary front doors for business formation exist including RVA Business Portal (launched January 2025), Virginia Business One Stop, departmental pages, and RVA 311 system, yet no single authoritative entry point guides users effectively. Front door proliferation creates choice overload and discovery barriers.

title: Services Landscape: Hidden Gaps
summary: Available services remain undiscovered due to poor information architecture, administrative barriers, and fragmentation across websites and departments. Intervention invisibility phenomenon suggests problem is not service availability but user access to knowledge of services.

title: Services Landscape: Duplication Risk
summary: Framework for distinguishing authentic gaps from assumed gaps in civic hackathon environments, emphasizing need for market validation before building competitive tools. Competitive landscape analysis prevents wasted effort on already-available services.

title: Data Inventory: Contracting
summary: Richmond procurement systems including iSupplier, OpenGov, Transparency Dashboard, and OMBD Supplier Diversity Management System provide structured data on contracts and vendors. Data availability varies by system with different access levels and update frequencies.

title: Data Inventory: Procurement Formats
summary: Socrata platform provides API access via SODA v2.1/v3.0 endpoints and OData to Richmond's City Contracts dataset in CSV format with BOM encoding. Technical specifications enable structured data integration for application development.

title: Data Inventory: Business Setup
summary: Comprehensive source inventory of municipal webpages, online portals, PDF forms, and regulatory documents exists across city and state agencies, but integration and curation remain manual. Data exists in disparate sources requiring aggregation for usable business formation guidance.

title: Data Inventory: Structured vs Manual
summary: Landscape distinguishes structured data availability (APIs, financial metrics, industry classification) from unstructured manual extraction (management assessment, competitive intelligence, customer feedback). Solution feasibility depends on data structure type available.

title: Data Inventory: Quality Assessment
summary: Data quality evaluation across six dimensions (completeness, accuracy, timeliness, validity, consistency, uniqueness) reveals documented issues in federal statistics, open data portals, and healthcare datasets. Quality issues constrain reliability of data-driven solutions.

title: Prior Art: Catalog
summary: Comparable tools catalog documents federal systems (SAM.gov, Small Business Search), municipal platforms (Accela, OpenGov, Granicus, ClearForms), and state/local portals providing reference patterns and lessons learned. Prior art analysis prevents reinvention and identifies proven design patterns.

title: Prior Art: Pattern Transfer
summary: Transferability frameworks examine core components, discretionary elements, and PIET-T model for contextual fit assessment of solutions from other jurisdictions. Framework guides decisions about which patterns transfer to Richmond's specific constraints.

title: Prior Art: Archetype Mapping
summary: Navigation solution archetypes include service navigators, AI-driven assistants, match tools, eligibility guides, workflow assistants, and public dashboards with different strengths in addressing distinct problem types. Archetype analysis guides solution selection.

title: Prior Art: Pitfalls
summary: Civic tech failures including design-reality gap, Jumo collapse ($62,221 value vs $3.5M investment), Ushahidi limitations, and e-voting failures demonstrate risks of overclaiming impact and misunderstanding deployment constraints. Pitfall analysis informs risk mitigation.

title: Prior Art: Weekend Viability
summary: Patterns for 48-hour rapid prototyping including 48-hour sprint framework, microteam composition, prototype design pattern, self-service automation, and event-driven architecture. Viability patterns guide realistic hackathon project scoping.

title: Opportunity Spaces: Ranked
summary: High-value software opportunities identified through research include healthcare data integration, financial services modernization, supply chain visibility, predictive maintenance, and government digital transformation. Ranking framework prioritizes feasibility and user impact.

title: Opportunity Spaces: MBE Framing
summary: Optimal framing for MBE contracting opportunities recommends proactive alerting integrated with guided next steps, with plain-language interpretation as secondary layer. Framing prioritizes action enablement over information display.

title: Opportunity Spaces: Setup Framing
summary: Seven framing approaches for small business navigation evaluated with hybrid guided intake combined with embedded decision trees recommended as optimal. Framing analysis balances structure with flexibility for diverse user needs.

title: Opportunity Spaces: Where Software Helps
summary: Software genuinely solves problems including scattered information consolidation, coordination at scale, cognitive load reduction, version/audit tracking, feedback loop latency, pattern spotting in noise, and reliable workflow sequencing. Software cannot fix misaligned incentives, undefined processes, trust gaps, or resource scarcity.

title: Opportunity Spaces: Do Not Build
summary: Strategic do-not-build list identifies initiatives exceeding organizational authority including regulatory compliance determinations, third-party data integration without permission, and unvalidated security/performance claims. Boundaries prevent overreach and liability.

title: Risks: AI-Heavy Solutions
summary: Critical risks in AI-heavy tools include hallucinations producing plausible-sounding false information, misleading eligibility advice with false confidence, stale data and model drift, and overclaiming prototype capabilities. AI risks compound accuracy and trust concerns in civic contexts.

title: Risks: Harm Cases
summary: Documented harm cases from AI systems include legal hallucinations inventing case citations, healthcare diagnostic models perpetuating racial bias, and insurance claim denials without clear explanation. Real-world failures demonstrate concrete risks of unguarded AI deployment.

title: Risks: Inclusion & Accessibility
summary: Inclusion and accessibility risks in digital solutions include digital literacy disparities (97% broadband access for white businesses vs 88% for BIPOC) and barriers for underserved populations. Technology solutions risk deepening existing inequities without deliberate accessibility design.

title: Risks: Matching Bias
summary: Algorithmic matching systems risk reinforcing existing competitive patterns, inadvertently favoring established businesses, and lacking transparency in matching logic. Matching bias analysis demonstrates how optimization can amplify inequality.

title: Risks: Practical Guardrails
summary: Practical safeguards for civic technology include human-in-the-loop decision frameworks, confidence score calibration, explicit uncertainty signaling, and bias detection strategies. Guardrails enable responsible AI deployment in high-stakes contexts.

title: MVP Feasibility: Shape
summary: Credible 48-hour MVP framework accommodates realistic constraints with 30-36 hours productive development available, distinguishing must-have functional components from acceptable mocked elements. Feasibility framework prevents overscoping and enables realistic goal-setting.

title: MVP Feasibility: MBE Public Data
summary: MVP feasibility for MBE procurement opportunity uses public data (Richmond contracts dataset, Virginia Business Opportunities, OMBD resources) without requiring system integration. Public data availability enables rapid prototyping without external dependencies.

title: MVP Feasibility: Setup Rule-Based
summary: MVP feasibility for business setup navigation operates using rule-based logic and decision trees applied to public business formation information. Rule-based approach avoids AI complexity while addressing navigation and sequencing problems.

title: MVP Feasibility: Minimums
summary: Minimum viable product definition identifies core value loops, ruthless prioritization of single hypothesis validation, and acceptance of manual workarounds. MVP minimums focus development on validating one specific user value claim.

title: MVP Feasibility: Scope Boundaries
summary: Scope boundaries framework for 48-hour MVPs defines target user segment, specific pain point, measurable outcome, and minimum viable path from awareness to value achievement. Boundaries discipline prevent feature creep and enable completion.

title: Demo Judging: Easiest
summary: Project archetypes demonstrate varying demo clarity with Paint-by-Numbers projects showing maximum clarity due to predictability, Film projects showing good clarity with clear vision, and Fog projects presenting significant communication challenges. Archetype analysis guides project selection for demo effectiveness.

title: Demo Judging: Credibility Evidence
summary: Evidence building for credibility includes past performance documentation, quantified user impact metrics, third-party validation, and risk management documentation. Credibility evidence transforms prototype claims into verifiable assertions.

title: Demo Judging: Prove Usefulness
summary: Demonstration strategies for proving usefulness include working prototypes, user test results, competitive comparison, and adoption metrics. Usefulness proof moves beyond feature demonstration to impact evidence.

title: Demo Judging: Best Archetypes
summary: Paint-by-Numbers and Film projects demonstrate most effectively in competitions due to superior demo clarity compared to Fog and Quest projects. Archetype selection directly impacts demo communication effectiveness and judge perception.

title: Demo Judging: Framing Language
summary: Framing language for demos emphasizes user problems, realistic scope, evidence of progress, and honest assessment of remaining work. Framing discipline builds judge confidence through transparent communication rather than overclaiming.
